coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), revolutionary carbon-based nanomaterial, have demonstrated tremendous potential for applications across multiple areas. Chitin-rich shrimp shells are a sustainable and renewable resource, and their utilization can turn waste into treasure. This work is dedicated to efficiently preparing nitrogen-doped green fluorescent CDs (N-CDs) for multi-modal applications from waste shrimp shells by hydrothermal methods. The detailed spectroscopic characterization reveals that the N-CDs possess excellent optical properties. The quantum yield is as high as 33.2%. Specifically, the N-CDs show excellent selectivity and sensitivity in the quantitative detection of Fe3+. Additionally, in response to pH changes, the surface functional groups of the N-CDs undergo protonation and deprotonation, leading to corresponding alterations in fluorescence colors. Capitalizing on this characteristic, a visual portable pH-measuring platform is developed. This platform ingeniously integrates nanoscale optical sensors with smartphone image processing technology, enabling real-time and highly accurate monitoring of environmental pH levels. Furthermore, they are ideal materials for fluorescence anti-counterfeiting detection due to their unique optical properties. This work simultaneously accomplishes the reuse of waste biomass and provides novel insights for multi-orbital applications, including metal ion detection, pH monitoring, and the anti-counterfeiting field.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), revolutionary carbon-based nanomaterial, have demonstrated tremendous potential for applications across multiple areas. Chitin-rich shrimp shells are a sustainable and renewable resource, and their utilization can turn waste into treasure. This work is dedicated to efficiently preparing nitrogen-doped green fluorescent CDs (N-CDs) for multi-modal applications from waste shrimp shells by hydrothermal methods. The detailed spectroscopic characterization reveals that the N-CDs possess excellent optical properties. The quantum yield is as high as 33.2%. Specifically, the N-CDs show excellent selectivity and sensitivity in the quantitative detection of Fe3+. Additionally, in response to pH changes, the surface functional groups of the N-CDs undergo protonation and deprotonation, leading to corresponding alterations in fluorescence colors. Capitalizing on this characteristic, a visual portable pH-measuring platform is developed. This platform ingeniously integrates nanoscale optical sensors with smartphone image processing technology, enabling real-time and highly accurate monitoring of environmental pH levels. Furthermore, they are ideal materials for fluorescence anti-counterfeiting detection due to their unique optical properties. This work simultaneously accomplishes the reuse of waste biomass and provides novel insights for multi-orbital applications, including metal ion detection, pH monitoring, and the anti-counterfeiting field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a new category of inorganic fluorescent materials, have garnered significant attention within the scientific community in recent years. These spherical carbon-containing nanomaterials, smaller than 10 nm, are high in sp2 carbon, bringing new life to the carbon material family. CDs inherit the advantages of low toxicity and good biocompatibility from carbon materials, but they also feature unique qualities such as controllable fluorescence (FL) emission, strong resistance to photobleaching, wide source of raw materials, and ease to synthesize on a large scale [1]. They are expected to be an excellent alternative to organic FL materials and have been extensively applied across a plethora of fields, including biosensors, optoelectronic devices, food safety assessment, and drug delivery systems [2–5]. Within these domains, the detection of metal ions, particularly Fe3+, assumes utmost importance. As a transition metal, Fe3+ plays an indispensable role in the human body, making substantial contributions to a wide array of biological processes. However, an imbalance—an excess or a deficiency—can severely damage the living system [6]. Hence, the development of methods capable of detecting Fe3+ with high sensitivity and selectivity within environmental and biological systems is of profound significance. CDs, endowed with FL capabilities, offer a straightforward approach to Fe3+ detection [7]. The pH value is crucial for environmental, biological, and chemical processes, serving as a key parameter for water-quality assessment and organism health [8]. In human biology, blood pH (7.35–7.45) and urine pH (4.5–8.0) are tightly regulated, with deviations indicating health issues [9]. In addition, pH is an important indicator for determining whether a body of water is normal and suitable for drinking as well as for aquaculture such as fish and shrimp [10]. Thus, the precise detection of pH is an ongoing and imperative need for environmental monitoring and the well-being of aquatic and human life. Additionally, CDs-based fluorescent anti-counterfeiting technology, a novel, and highly reliable anti-counterfeiting solution, is garnering increasing attention from various sectors [11]. However, as technology marches forward inexorably and practical applications continue to evolve ceaselessly, the ever-growing complexity and diversity in the application domain necessitate that CDs be able to meet multi-dimensional demands. Thus, the exploration of CDs' multi-mode applications has become an urgent imperative for maintaining environmental safety and safeguarding human and animal health.

In the field of CDs preparation, the choice of precursors is of crucial importance. Traditional synthesis methods often utilize specific compounds containing nitrogen, sulfur, and other doping elements as precursors [12,13]. Although CDs with acceptable performance can be fabricated through these approaches, issues such as raw material costs and sustainability are confronted. Shrimp shells, regarded as waste products, have come into the spotlight at this juncture [14–17]. Shrimp, a widely cultivated creature in both marine and freshwater, is highly processed in the food industry, and a lot of shrimp shells are produced as a by-product. Some of the shrimp shells are utilized to make chitin, chitosan, feed additives, fertilizer or extract its effective constituents like protein and astaxanthin [18–21]. However, a majority of shrimp shells are discarded as waste. With the rapid advancement of materials science, their worth is being redefined from ‘waste’ to ‘treasure’. Shrimp shells appear to be useless, but they contain plenty of organic macromolecules and their polymers, particularly chitin. Chitin, a natural polysaccharide found in shrimp shells, is the world's second second-largest biopolymer after cellulose [22–24]. The chemical structure of chitin is very similar to that of plant cellulose. The basic unit of chitin is acetylglucosamine and the molecular weight is more than one million [17,25]. During the preparation of CDs, the chitin in shrimp shells can not only be used as an excellent carbon (C) source, but also provide a nitrogen (N) source, furnishing a highly favorable precursor for the production of CDs. When juxtaposed with traditional precursors like breadcrumbs [26], white pepper [27], and tangerines [28], shrimp shells exhibit remarkable advantages. The synthesis of CDs from shrimp shells represents a remarkable transformation of waste into high-value materials. This process not only meets the essential requirements of sustainable development but also effectively reduces pollution levels while enabling resource recycling. It also opens up a new way of tackling the long-standing problem of shrimp shell disposal. Furthermore, the natural constituents within shrimp shells endow CDs with unique characteristics. For example, the N in chitin can facilitate N-doping in CDs, thereby altering the electronic structure and optical properties of the CDs. However, considering the limitation of N content in shrimp shells, CDs synthesized directly from biomass often face problems such as weak FL intensity.

Therefore, in view of the substantial amount of chitin present in shrimp shells, discarded shrimp shells were utilized as the raw material in the study. Through the introduction of m-phenylenediamine (MPD) as an N source supplement, multifunctional N-doped bright green fluorescent CDs (N-CDs) with specific properties were fabricated. The N-CDs exhibited a photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of up to 33.2%, signifying good performance in the field of optical properties. As depicted in Fig. 1 , these N-CDs boasted remarkable performance in the detection of Fe3+, characterized by high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. The detection limit (LOD) of Fe3+ is 0.66 μM. Moreover, by integrating the N-CDs optical sensor technology with smartphone image processing technology, a visual low-cost portable pH measurement platform was constructed. Surprisingly, thanks to their unique FL properties and excellent light stability, the N-CDs could also serve as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting inks. To the best of our knowledge, there has been no reported work that utilized CDs derived from identical precursors for these three applications simultaneously. This work not only offers a valuable reference for the preparation of waste biomass-based CDs but also unveils novel possibilities and solutions regarding metal ion detection, environmental pH detection, and FL anti-counterfeiting.

Shrimp shells were obtained from the canteen of Guangxi Seafood Processing Plant, China. After being cleaned with water, the leftover shrimp shells were oven-dried for 24 h at 50 °C. Ground dried shrimp shells into a powder and stored at room temperature for backup. MPD was acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. HCl, NaOH, C6H8O7.H2O (0.1 M), and Na2HPO4.2H2O (0.2 M) were sourced from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The other metal ions in the selective experiments were all in the form of chloride salts provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dialysis bags were produced by Hunan Yibo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Deionized water was employed during the entire process.

The structures and particle sizes were characterized through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (FEI Tecnai F20, USA). The microstructure was analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (SU8020, Japan). Additionally, the energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) patterns were accomplished with the use of the same device to explore sample elemental species and distribution. The X-ray diffraction pattern was determined by X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (DX2700A, Japan) operating with a scanning speed of 10 °/min from 10° to 80°. The components of the samples were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific K-AlpHa Nexsa, USA). The UV–Vis absorption spectra were carried out with the UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) (Perkin Elmer, USA) was conducted to analyze functional groups, and the testing procedure was based on previous work [29]. FL spectra were measured by FL spectrophotometer (F90 Pro, Shimadzu, Japan) according to the method of [30]. The FL lifetime was evaluated with a quantum yield spectrometer (FLS 1000, UK). Zeta potential values were acquired with the aid of Zeta potential analyzer (NANO ZS90X, Malvern, UK). The FL color change of N-CDs was observed through an ultraviolet analyzer (Qiwei, China).

N-CDs were synthesized as follows: 1.5 g of pre-treated shrimp shell powder and 0.75 g of MPD were mixed into 20 mL of deionized water. After 10 min of stirring and 5 min of sonication, transfer the mixture to a PTFE-lined autoclave and conduct the reaction at 190 °C for 16 h to obtain a brownish-yellow solution. Then, centrifuge the solution at 4000 rpm for 5 min to eliminate larger insoluble particles. The top solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane. The resultant filtrate was dialyzed using a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h to remove unreacted materials and contaminants, yielding an N-CDs solution. Ultimately, the solution was freeze-dried, yielding N-CDs solid powder at 6.5 wt%. The N-CDs powder was configured as a standard solution (2 mg/mL), chilled at 4 °C and protected from light for subsequent characterization and experiments. CDs were synthesized without the addition of MPD under similar conditions.

To achieve more precise and sensitive Fe3+ detection, the effects of diverse solvents, pH and response time on the detection system were investigated, and the best detection settings were identified. Under these optimal conditions, 200 μL of N-CDs solution with different a range of Fe3+ (0–800 μM) was diluted to 5 mL by adding pH 2 buffer and thoroughly shaken. After standing for 3 min, the FL emission spectra were documented (EX = 400 nm). Based on the obtained data, corresponding working curves were drawn. A detailed analysis was carried out to assess the specificity and anti-interference abilities of N-CDs for common ions. The 16 common cations (Zn2+, Cu2+, In3+, Al3+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ba2+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, Li+, Co2+, Sr2+, Fe2+, Fe3+) and 13 anions (HPO4 2−, Cl−, F−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, OH−, S2−, SO3 2−, MoO4 −, HCO3 −, ClO2 −, IO3 −) were determined. In this process, 200 μL of N-CDs solution was taken and mixed with 300 μL (10 mM) of each of the ions. The complicated solution was then dredged to 5 mL with pH 2 and left to stand for 3 min. Subsequently, FL emission spectra were recorded and analyzed under consistent conditions. Moreover, to verify the anti-interference ability of these N-CDs for Fe3+ detection, the same volume of Fe3+ (10 mM) was added to the system and well-shaken to ensure homogeneity. The FL emission spectrums were documented under the same conditions for analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Locally collected tap water, drinking water, and lake water specimens served as test specimens for detecting Fe3+. Prior to FL analysis, all specimens were filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, they were diluted threefold with deionized water. The reliability and accuracy of the N-CDs-based FL probe in detecting Fe3+ were confirmed through spiked recovery experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and color response of N-CDs to pH
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of N-CDs to pH was investigated. To begin, the pH of the solution was regulated to the demanded parameter using HC1(0.1 M) and NaOH (0.1 M) as acid-base adjusters. Next, 200 μL of N-CDs solution was added to 4.8 mL of various pH buffers and allowed to stand for 3 min. Finally, the FL emission spectra of various pH systems were scanned and recorded, with working curves plotted (EX = 400 nm). Further photographs were taken under 365 nm UV light using a smartphone. A color recognition application (color picker APP) installed on the smartphone captured a series of fluorescent images and output the corresponding RGB values to complete the quantitative assay.

Paper with a non-fluorescent background was selected as the drawing paper, and a standard concentration of N-CDs solution was inhaled with a pen to trace the pattern on the paper. After the paper was dried at room temperature, it was placed in a dark room and irradiated with 365 nm UV light, and a camera was used to take FL imaging photographs.

The FL intensity of N-CDs was used as a reference index to explore the optimum conditions for the preparation of N-CDs. N-CDs were fabricated by hydrothermal approach, where shrimp shell powder was utilized as a C source and MPD functioned as an additional N source. The luminescence properties of N-CDs were susceptible to several synthetic factors, such as the types of the additional C and N sources, the dosage of the additional C and N sources, hydrothermal reaction temperature, and reaction time. Therefore, to synthesize N-CDs with the strongest FL intensity, the above synthesis conditions need to be optimized first. As illustrated in Fig. S1A, N-CDs were synthesized under the same experimental circumstances using shrimp shell powder and various additives. When MPD was employed, the produced N-CDs' FL intensity peaked at 500 nm. As depicted in Fig. S1B, with the mass of shrimp shell powder maintained at 1.5 g and varying amounts of DMF, it was found that the FL intensity of the synthesized N-CDs reached the maximum when the amount of MPD was 0.75 g. The FL intensity of the synthesized N-CDs was the strongest at 500 nm when the MPD was used. The experiment also investigated how the temperature of the hydrothermal reaction affected the N-CDs' FL intensity. As illustrated in Fig. S1C, under identical reaction conditions except for reaction temperature, the maximum FL intensity of the synthesized N-CDs was achieved at a reaction temperature of 190 °C. Finally, the impact of reaction duration on the FL intensity of the synthesized N-CDs was evaluated. Fig. S1D illustrated that the FL intensity of the synthesized N-CDs peaked after 16 h of reaction. According to the experimental findings, the ideal conditions for the synthesis of N-CDs were as follows: the shrimp shell powder of 1.5 g as raw C source, 0.75 g of MPD as the additive N source, 20 mL of deionized water as hydrothermal reagent, the reaction temperature was 190 °C, and the reaction time was 16 h.

The following was a putative N-CDs production pathway under hydrothermal conditions. Chitin in waste shrimp shells was a N-containing polymer composed of N-acetyl-D- glucosamine connected by β-1,4 glycosidic linkages. The hydrothermal environment's high temperatures and pressures excited water molecules, which operated as nucleophiles at the molecular level, attacking glycosidic bonds and breaking them. The chitin macromolecule was thus broken down into minute pieces comprising C chains and N-containing groups such as acetylamino groups. Under hydrothermal circumstances, MPD took part in the reaction. Its amino groups reacted with chitin breakdown fragments to join C chains. MPD molecules might also have reacted to generate N-containing conjugated intermediates. As the reaction progressed, chitin degradation fragments decomposed and recombined. N-containing groups—both naturally occurring in chitin and those added by MPD—had chemical linkages that were continuously modified. The synthesis of the initial N-CDs structure was aided by the formation of new CC bonds between C atoms and the participation of N-containing groups in the creation of new structures. C structural intermediates containing N polymerized at high temperatures and high pressures. CC bonds were used to create larger carbon skeletons. By doping them with nitrogen atoms from MPD and those left over from chitin breakdown, N-CDs' electronic structure was altered, their active sites and conductivity were increased, and covalent bonds like pyrrole N and graphitic N were formed with nearby C atoms. C atoms on the surface of N-CDs reacted with small molecules in the hydrothermal environment to form functional groups like -OH and -COOH in the later stage of the hydrothermal reaction. This stabilized the structure and gave it chemical activity and water solubility, ultimately resulting in stable N-CDs. During the procedure, MPD contributed another source of N and worked in concert with the byproducts of chitin degradation to generate N-CDs [31–35].

Fig. 2A presented the TEM image of the synthesized N-CDs. This image revealed that the morphologies of the N-CDs predominantly consisted of spherical dots, which were well-dispersed and exhibited a uniform size. In the HRTEM image depicted in Fig. 2B, distinct lattice stripes were observable on the N-CDs. These stripes were accurately fitted, resulting in a lattice stripe spacing of 0.22 nm. This value corresponded to the (100) crystalline phase of graphite, suggesting that the N-CDs had undergone a certain degree of graphitization during the preparation process [36]. The average diameter of the N-CDs was measured to be 2.26 ± 0.59 nm, with the majority of the particles ranging from 2.0 to 3.0 nm (Fig. 2C). The average scale of the CDs, as demonstrated in Fig. 2D, was approximately 3.70 ± 0.96 nm. Evidently, introducing the N element led to a substantial reduction in the particle size of the N-CDs. As illustrated in the SEM image in Figs. S2A and B, the structure of the shrimp shell had been completely decomposed due to hydrothermal treatment. This further corroborated the formation of quasi-spherical N-CDs [37]. Additionally, SEM-EDS was conducted on the samples. The EDS spectra of the fabricated N-CDs indicated that the samples contained C, N, and O, distributed uniformly. Significantly, the N content of the N-CDs was markedly higher than that of the CDs, indicating the successful incorporation of the N element from the MPD additives into the N-CDs (Fig. 2E, F).

Furthermore, a large peak near 2θ = 20° was visible in the XRD spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 3A), corresponding with the graphitic carbon (002) plane and demonstrating the successful preparation of the N-CDs [30].To delve deeply into the structural alterations of N-CDs, a comprehensive surface analysis was carried out using FT-IR spectra and XPS of samples prepared with and without MPD. As depicted in Fig. 3B, the FT-IR spectra indicate that all samples are rich in O-containing and N-containing functional groups. Specifically, the broad absorption peaks between 3221 cm−1-3537 cm−1 could be attributed to the OH and NH stretching vibrations. Meanwhile, the C=O/C=N stretching vibration has a significant absorption peak at 1632 cm−1, while the planar bending vibration of O-H/N-H and the stretching vibration of CO and C-O-C were also more significant at about 600 cm−1, 1412 cm−1 and 1107 cm−1, respectively [38]. Overall, no significant changes were observed in the peak positions of N-CDs prepared with the introduction of MPD or CDs prepared without the introduction of MPD. However, the variations in peak intensities were remarkably pronounced. For instance, when MPD was not introduced, although a broad absorption peak also emerged in the infrared spectrum of the prepared CDs within the range of 3221 cm−1-3537 cm−1, its signal intensity was extremely weak. This is highly likely due to the relatively lower N content in shrimp shells than that in MPD, which renders it difficult for the signals of relevant functional groups to be fully manifested. Additionally, the peak intensities at 1632 cm−1 and 600 cm−1 increased with the introduction of MPD. This phenomenon not only indicated that the contents of NH and CN groups increased with the rise in N content but also implied that the conjugation degree of N-CDs was enhanced accordingly, ultimately resulting in longer wavelengths [39,40]. The as-generated N-CDs with different surface chemistries and morphologies, which are crucial for their performance [41]. XPS was employed to conduct an in-depth exploration of the chemical composition and elemental valence states of the samples. In the full-spectrum XPS of N-CDs, three distinct characteristic peaks were observed. Their central positions were located at 285.92 eV, 401.54 eV, and 533.16 eV, respectively (Fig. 3C). These peaks were ascribed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, with atomic ratios of 51.23%, 14.84%, and 33.93%, respectively. Furthermore, by comparing the N-atom percentage in the CDs shown in Fig. 3D, it was merely 7.74%. In the XPS analysis, the atomic percentage of N in the samples prepared with MPD was significantly higher, further confirming the contribution of MPD. All samples have three distinctive peaks that corresponded to the C=C/C-C, C-N/C-O, and C=O/C=N motifs that were visible in the high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 3E) [42]. The N1s spectra provide the form of N present in N-CD, showing the presence of graphite N peak (399.97 eV) and pyrrole N peak (399.16 eV) (Fig. 3F). It was also found that the N-doped graphite N content was significantly higher than that of the undoped N. The doping of graphite N can greatly reduce the bandgap, and thus they can significantly reduce the HOMO-LOMO bandgap, which reduces the energy of the corresponding optical leaps, which may be the reason for the redshift of the N-CDs [43]. The O1s spectra (Fig. 3G) showed two peaks at 531.25 eV and 532.50 eV, which were in line with the distinctive peaks of the CO and C-OH/C-O-C groups [44]. After XPS and FT-IR analyses, these results indicate the successful doping of N elements and many -NH2 and -COOH were bonded on the surface of N-CDs. Generally, the N-CDs surface was rich in hydrophilic functional groups, supporting its exceptional water solubility.

Additionally, the optical characteristics of N-CDs were further uncovered by the UV–Vis absorption spectra, FL emission and excitation spectra investigation. The N-CDs showed two separate absorption spikes in the UV–Vis absorption spectra, which were situated at 220 nm and 295 nm, respectively. The π-π* jumps of the conjugated skeleton and n-π* leaps of heteroatom-containing bonds are primarily responsible for the formation of the two peaks (Fig. 3H) [45,46]. In natural light, N-CDs that were dissolved in water looked pale yellow, but when exposed to 365 nm UV lamp irradiation, they fluoresced green (as seen in the inset of Fig. 3H). This finding implied that the emission from the defective state linked to the heteroatom-containing bonds of N-CDs may be the source of their luminescence [47]. Various excitation wavelengths were used to examine the wavelength dependence of N-CDs. When the excitation wavelength was changed between 310 nm and 490 nm, N-CDs displayed an excitation wavelength dependence, as seen in Fig. 3I. This was mostly explained by the fact that N-CDs have several functional groups (such as -COOH, -NH2, and -OH) on their surface that can produce various energy bands. The position of N-CDs' FL emission peak was redshifted from 438 nm to 530 nm, with a redshift of 92 nm, as a result of the change in excitation wavelength (see inset Fig. 3I). The N-CDs' surface flaws and fluctuating particle sizes could be the cause of this behavior [48,49]. When the excitation wavelength was 400 nm, N-CDs showed the strongest FL, while their ideal emission wavelength was 500 nm. The QY of N-CDs was resolved to be 33.2% and CDs to be 9.7% (for details of the calculations, see Text S1) [50]. These values are the equivalent or improved from previously published QY values for CDs manufacturing using biomass waste (Table S1).

Furthermore, FL stability is a crucial metric for evaluating N-CDs performance. The FL stability of N-CDs was examined from six perspectives: temperature, storage duration, UV light irradiation, concentrations of oxidation, oxidation time and tolerance of salt. According to the results, N-CDs' FL intensity was constant throughout a range of ionic concentrations, indicating good salt tolerance (Fig. 4A). Meanwhile, the FL intensity of N-CDs maintained nearly unchanged after 60 min of continuous UV illumination (Fig. 4B), indicating excellent photobleaching resistance. When the temperature changed from 20 °C to 90 °C, the FL intensity of N-CDs was relatively stable, demonstrating remarkable thermal resistance (Fig. 4C). After 60 days of storage, although there was a slight decrease, the FL intensity of N-CDs stood stable at a high level, proving good storage stability (Fig. 4D). Eventually, to investigate the antioxidant properties of N-CDs, the FL intensity of N-CDs solutions at various concentrations of H₂O₂ (0–200 μM) was measured and presented almost no change (Fig. 4E). Additionally, the FL intensity of N-CDs solutions in the existence of H₂O₂ (100 μM) within 60 min changed little (Fig. 4F). The results of Fig. 4E, F showed that N-CDs possess good antioxidant properties. In conclusion, the synthesized N-CDs exhibit good photostability. These characteristics confer N-CDs tremendous potential for application in the field of fluorescent probes and are expected to provide efficient fluorescent materials for practical uses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of Fe3+ FL sensor based on N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal detection conditions were explored before performing the Fe3+ detection. The three aspects of the solvent type, the solution pH and the reaction time were optimized to enhance the performance of N-CDs for the detection of Fe3+. The highest FL intensity of N-CDs was obtained when H₂O was used as the solvent, and the emission region was in the green wavelength range. In various organic solvents, the as-synthesized N-CDs showed diverse FL emission characteristics (Fig. 5A). N-CDs frequently exhibited excitation wavelength-dependent behavior in organic solvents. This variation stems from their different particle sizes and the presence of many functional groups (-COOH, -OH, and CO) in their structures, which may lead to varying emission positions [51,52]. Fig. 5B showed that the FL intensity of N-CDs peaked at a solution pH 2. Analyzing the data in Fig. 5C, the FL intensity of Fe3+ was close to the equilibrium when Fe3+ was added to the system incubating about 1 min and remaining stable for 15 min. This indicated that the reaction of N-CDs with Fe3+ was accomplished within 1 min, forming a stable complex. To ensure the accuracy of the results, the optimal reaction time was determined to be 3 min. In conclusion, the optimal conditions for the detection of Fe3+ in this system were as follows: buffer solution with pH 2 was used as the detection medium, and the equilibrium time was kept for 3 min after the addition of Fe3+.

The FL intensity of N-CDs decreased sequentially when the concentration of Fe3+ was increased from 0 μM to 800.0 μM under the above optimal experimental conditions, which displayed the typical concentration-dependent behavior (Fig. 5D). There was no discernible effect in the presence of natural light (see inset in Fig. 5D). The standard deviation of the detection results at the same Fe3+ concentration is small, and the reproducibility of N-CDs is good, which lays the foundation for the subsequent experiments. As shown in Fig. 5E, the quenching efficiency of FL intensity F/F0 was shown to be a corresponding function of Fe3+ concentration. The quenching efficiencies of FL intensity and Fe3+ concentration were linearly related when the Fe3+ concentration was in the intervals of 0–20 μM and 20–800 μM, and the fitting equations were (1) F/F0 = −0.33202CFe 3+ + 100.131, R2 = 0.9970; and (2) F/F0 = −0.0446CFe 3+ + 94.32752, R2 = 0.9986, indicating high correlation. According to the formula LOD = 3δ/d (where δ is the standard deviation of the FL intensity of the blank samples, n = 6; d is the slope of the linear fitting equation), the LOD of the fluorescent probe was determined to be 0.66 μM using the LOD equation as the standard detection curve [53]. Compared with other detection methods (as shown in Table S2), the analytical parameters were comparable with or even better, providing a novel and promising approach for the detection of Fe3+. The results demonstrated the high feasibility of using as-synthesized N-CDs for Fe3+ residue detection [54].

The assay's specific response and anti-interference capability are essential requirements for detecting targets in complicated samples. To comprehensively evaluate the performance of this method, the response of N-CDs to Fe3+ was tested for 16 common cations and 13 anions. As shown in Fig. 5F, Fe3+ has a remarkable burst effect on the FL of N-CDs, with a 60% reduction in FL intensity, highlighting the specific responsiveness of N-CDs to Fe3+. Other ions had negligible interactions with the FL intensity of N-CDs and no significant responsiveness. The FL intensity of N-CDs did not change significantly when other ions were added, indicating little FL response to these ions. N-CDs' outstanding anti-interference ability was demonstrated by the fact that their FL intensity could still be successfully burst after Fe3+ was added again (Figs. 5H, I). In conclusion, the fluorescent probes based on N-CDs proposed in this work have significant advantages in the detection of Fe3+, with highly specific responses and excellent anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL sensing mechanism of Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the FL quenching process describes the interactions between fluorophores and quencher molecules, which is typically driven by various mechanisms including dynamic quenching (DQE), static quenching (SQE), internal filtration effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and FL resonance energy transfer (FRET) [55–58]. To distinguish these mechanisms, observing the changes in UV–Vis absorption spectra and FL lifetime decay between N-CDs and quenchers is an extremely crucial method. First, the FL lifetime changes of N-CDs in the existence and exclusion of Fe3+ were examined. As illustrated in Fig. 6A, the average lifetimes of N-CDs with and without the addition of Fe3+ were 2.66 ns and 2.45 ns, respectively, showing a 7% FL lifetime decay. This minor change confirmed that DQE does not play a dominant role in the process. Additionally, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of N-CDs at different Fe3+ concentrations revealed that the absorbance of the N-CDs solution gradually increased with the addition of Fe3+. No new absorption peaks emerged, indicating that no new compounds were generated between N-CDs and Fe3+ (Fig. 6B). Notably, the FL excitation peaks of N-CDs partially overlapped with the UV–Vis absorption peaks of Fe3+ but did not overlap with the emission peaks (Fig. 6C). This may imply that Fe3+ absorbs the energy of the excitation wavelength part of N-CDs, resulting in a reduction in their emission intensity, which is typical of IFE [59]. To further investigate the FL quenching mechanism of Fe3+ on N-CDs, the Zeta potentials of N-CDs, Fe3+, and the mixtures of N-CDs and Fe3+ at pH 2 were determined (Fig. 6D). The results indicated that the potentials of the mixtures were significantly different from those of N-CDs and Fe3+, suggesting the existence of electrostatic interactions between Fe3+ and N-CDs [60]. Meanwhile, the positive potentials of N-CDs and the negative potentials of the mixtures indicated that Fe3+ mainly interacts with the positively charged groups on the appearance of N-CDs [61]. The -NH2 on the surface of N-CDs was found to be closely related to their ability to quench Fe3+, based on FT-IR and XPS characterization data analysis effectively. Because Fe3+ has multiple empty orbits, the abundance of -NH2 on the surface of the N-CDs provides an electron donor for the lone pair of electrons on the surface of the N-CDs with transferring to the unfilled orbitals of Fe3+, leading to non-radiative electron/hole complexation and ultimately quenching the FL of the N-CDs [13,58,62,63]. In summary, the FL quenching mechanism of N-CDs by Fe3+ was the collaborative result of the IFE and PET synergistic effect, as illustrated in Fig. 6E.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the practical applicability of the method for real sample detection, the fluorescent probe technique employing N-CDs was applied for detecting Fe3+ in aquatic environment. The standard addition method of recovery was implemented to quantify Fe3+ in lake water, tap water, and domestic drinking water samples. Fe3+ with the different concentrations after 3-fold dilution was added to the water samples and the detection results were meticulously recorded, as presented in Table 1 . From the data within the table, it was evident that the recovery of Fe3+ by this method demonstrated excellent performance, which ranged from 100.49% to 104.25%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) did not exceed 3.51%. Simultaneously, to authenticate the reliability of the detection results, we verified the results of the assay by o-phenanthroline spectrophotometry method. As discernible from the data in Table 1, the detection results obtained by the two methods were remarkably similar. This indicated that the method proposed in this study for detecting Fe3+ content in the aqueous environment possessed high reliability. In conclusion, this method indicates the potential of N-CDs to detect Fe3+ in realistic samples and offering new possibilities and options for the assay of Fe3+ in aquatic environments.

Examination of the responsiveness of N-CDs-based probes to pH was performed by adjusting the solution's pH. As depicted in Fig. 7A, the FL intensity of N-CDs increased with the pH value changing from 1 to 2. As the pH value was changed from 2 to 13, the FL intensity of N-CDs significantly dropped. Meanwhile, the FL quenching efficiency F/F0 for various pH values gradually increased, whereas the FL intensity of N-CDs at 500 nm progressively decreased (inset of Fig. 7A). As illustrated in Fig. 7B, there was a linear relationship between pH ranging from 2.0 to 13 and the degree of FL quenching in the system. The linear equation was F/F0 = 1.20123–0.07219pH, with R2 of 0.9911. By analyzing the maximum FL intensity as a function of pH [8,64], the pKa of N-CDs was 7.85 (Fig. S3), which is between the pH values of real water [65]. The result illustrated that N-CDs had the potential application value in actual water samples. The changes of N-CDs exposed to natural light and 365 nm UV illumination in different pH environments were photographed in Figs. 7C, D respectively. The figures showed that as the surrounding pH value varies, the color of the N-CDs solution changed from light yellow and ultimately to colorless under natural light. Significant FL intensity and color changes were observed during UV light exposure. Next, the solution pH was frequently shifted between 2 and 13 to assess N-CDs' reversible response to pH detection. As demonstrated in Fig. 7E, the probe's FL intensity rapidly increased at pH 2 and subsequently swiftly reduced at pH 13, suggesting that the probe N-CDs had strong FL reversibility in the different pH solutions and could be utilized to monitor pH changes in real-time. The results indicated that N-CDs can function as fluorescent nanoprobes for visual real-time pH detection in an aqueous solution. The N-CDs probe provided dual-mode detection of pH in both colorimetric and fluorescent modes. However, although there was a significant color change under UV irradiation, it was difficult for the human eye to distinguish the details, and accurate quantitative analysis was impossible. For visualization and quantitative analysis, pH was quantified using red (R), green (G), and blue (B) colorimetry, and a visual portable sensing platform that combined proportional fluorescent nanoprobes with smartphones was constructed. Under 365 nm UV light, photographs of N-CDs solutions in diverse pH environmental conditions were captured using a smartphone (iPhone 14). Subsequently, the RGB value parameters of these images were meticulously analyzed with the aid of the smartphone application “Color Recognition Picker”. A good correlation between the G/B ratio and pH value, the equation of y = 0.96767 + (14.2249-0.96767)/1 + exp((x + 2.056061)/dx), with R2 = 0.9971 was revealed (Fig. 7F). Therefore, the development of a portable pH detection platform suitable for smartphones, featuring visualizability, quantitative analysis, and high sensitivity, demonstrated great application potential. The probe constructed based on N-CDs innovatively achieved two pH-value detection modes, namely RGB colorimetry and fluorometry. Compared with the previously reported traditional pH detection methods [8,38,66], this approach had significant advantages. They possessed excellent reversibility, indicating their reusability. The multi-mode detection mechanism effectively avoided the generation of false-positive signals, greatly enhancing the accuracy and reliability of the detection results. Moreover, the detection platform was lightweight and highly visual, which could perfectly meet the practical demands of on-site, real-time, and rapid detection. This would provide more convenient tools for daily pH detection and research in related fields.

Using a UV–vis spectrophotometer and potential tester, a series of measurements and experiments were conducted to investigate in detail the causes of the variations in N-CDs FL intensity at various pH values. These experiments and measurements provided information to hypothesize whether these variations are connected to the internal structure and electrical peculiarities of the N-CDs surface. N-CDs have an absorption peak at 220 nm in acidic, neutral, or alkaline aqueous environments by analysis of the data in the UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 7G). Notably, N-CDs had an extra absorption peak at 420 nm in an acidic environment. According to relevant investigations, this absorption peak was due to an electron jumping from the surface defect state to the ground state, suggesting that N-CDs may undergo a protonation reaction in acidic conditions. Protonation increases the number of active charges on the surface of N-CDs, resulting in an absorption peak at a longer wavelength [67–70]. The electrical properties of N-CDs at various pH levels were ascertained using a zeta potential test to verify this deduction (Fig. 7H). The findings further verified that N-CDs exhibited relatively high Zeta potentials in acidic environments, reconfirming that N-CDs undergo protonation in acidic media. Moreover, it was observed that the intensity of the absorption peaks tended to decrease as pH increased, indicating an achromatic effect. This can be explained by the fact that electron leaps become more challenging when pH rises due to the band gap widening and decreased conjugation action of N-CDs. This further led to the phenomenon known as the blue shift of the peaks, or it could be the result of a decrease in charge density [71,72]. By tracking the change in N-CDs' Zeta potential with pH, it was found that the Zeta potential of N-CDs gradually decreased from an acidic to an alkaline media. The findings verified the decrease in charge density and further suggested that the blue-shift phenomena in the peaks were caused by both charge density and electron transport [73]. In conclusion, protonation appears to be the primary cause of the fluctuations of FL of N-CDs aqueous solution in varying pH values, based on the experimental results and speculations (Fig. 7I). This discovery is crucial for understanding the physicochemical characteristics of N-CDs and their applicability in various scenarios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CDs to pH in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further demonstrate the practical applicability of N-CDs, we evaluated their ability to monitor pH levels in real-world water samples (lake water, tap water, and drinking water). This initiative was designed to extend their practical applications further. The samples underwent a pre-treatment process, where they were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min and then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. Subsequently, they were formulated into test solutions with diverse pH values, specifically 6.2, 6.6, 7.0, and 7.4. The detection results were documented, as demonstrated in Table 2 . An outstanding recovery rate, from 97.43% to 104.14%, was attained. Additionally, the RSD was found to be less than 3.05%. These findings unequivocally indicated that the detection results derived from this method manifested high stability and reliability.

Counterfeiting has emerged as an escalating global issue, exerting far-reaching impacts on consumers, businesses, and governments alike. It not only incurs substantial economic losses but also poses health risks. There is a pressing need to utilize inexpensive, uncomplicated, and stable fluorescent materials for message encryption to safeguard documents, and banknotes from counterfeiting [74,75]. The as-synthesized N-CDs were employed as fluorescent ink for handcrafted patterns. The fluorescent paintings were drawn on non-fluorescent paper, dried naturally after completion, and then imaged fluorescently with a camera. The fluorescent inks composed of the as-synthesized N-CDs performed well as could be presented in Figs. 8A, B. Patterns made with N-CDs were not obvious under normal daylight, but emit a strong green FL that was visible to the naked eye under a 365 nm UV lamp. This phenomenon highlights the special FL properties of N-CDs and verifies their feasibility as a cost-effective alternative to conventional FL inks. In addition, Fig. 8C showed that the pattern was still legible after three months. Given the long-lasting nature of N-CDs, their application in the printing industry is promising, both for traditional printing requirements and for cutting-edge applications such as encryption and data storage.

From environmental protection, renewable energy, and sustainable development perspectives, this work employed chitin-rich waste shrimp shells as the raw material and MPD as the N-source additive. A simple and environmentally friendly one-step hydrothermal technique enabled the effective synthesis of N-CDs. Systematic characterization indicated that the synthesized N-CDs were spherical, possessed abundant surface functional groups, and were hydrophilic. N doping significantly augmented various properties of N-CDs, such as elevating the QY from 9.7% to 33.2% and bolstering chemical stability. N-CDs functioned as effective ion fluorescent probes, pH sensors, and fluorescent security inks. Specifically, for detecting Fe3+, the limit of detection was 0.66 μM and had high selectivity. This was due to the IEF and PET quenching mechanisms, making them efficient probes for Fe3+ detection. The viability of the N-CDs probe was successfully verified using actual samples from diverse water sources including drinking water, lake water, and tap water. Moreover, in accordance with the protonation process, the surface functional groups of N-CDs protonated and deprotonated in response to pH variations, thereby changing the FL intensity. Based on this, a mobile-phone-based intelligent visual portable pH measurement platform has been successfully developed, achieving high-accuracy real-time monitoring of environmental acidity and alkalinity. Thanks to their unique FL properties, N-CDs could also be used as fluorescent ink for writing and drawing applications, paving the way for anti-counterfeiting. This work presents a rarely seen strategy of simultaneous three-channel applications of N-CDs derived from waste biomass shrimp shells. It also provides a new strategy for the preparation of N-CDs with long wavelength emission, but also extends their applications in environmental monitoring and safety.
